questionText: The statement $$\sim[p \vee(\sim(p \wedge q))]$$ is equivalent to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline p & q & p \wedge q & \sim(p \wedge q) & \begin{array}{c} p \vee \sim \\ (p \wedge q) \end{array} & \begin{array}{l} \sim[p \vee \sim \\ (p \wedge q)] \end{array} & p \vee q & \begin{array}{l} \sim(p \\ \vee q) \end{array} & \begin{array}{l} (p \wedge q) \wedge \\ \quad(\sim p) \end{array} \\ \hline \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} \\ \hline \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} \\ \hline \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} \\ \hline \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} \\ \hline \end{array} $$

$$ \therefore \sim[p \vee(\sim(p \wedge q))]=\sim p \wedge(p \wedge q) $$

questionText: The negation of the statement $$(p \vee q) \wedge (q \vee ( \sim r))$$ is :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The negation of the statement $(p \vee q) \wedge(q \vee(\sim r))$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sim[(p \vee q) \wedge(q \vee(\sim r))] \\\\ & =\sim[(p \vee q) \vee \sim(q \vee(\sim r)] \\\\ & =((\sim p) \wedge \sim q)) \vee((\sim q) \wedge r)) \end{aligned} $$

Apply distribution law,

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\sim q \wedge((\sim p) \vee r)) \\\\ & =((\sim p) \vee r) \wedge(\sim q) \end{aligned} $$

questionText: The statement $$(p \wedge(\sim q)) \vee((\sim p) \wedge q) \vee((\sim p) \wedge(\sim q))$$ is equivalent to _________.
subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \mathbf{p} & \mathbf{q} & \sim \mathbf{q} & \sim \mathbf{p} & \mathbf{p} \wedge \sim \mathbf{q} & \mathbf{\sim p} \wedge \mathbf{q} & \mathbf{\sim p} \wedge \mathbf{\sim q} \\ \hline T & T & F & F & F & F & F \\ T & F & T & F & T & F & F \\ F & T & F & T & F & T & F \\ F & F & T & T & F & F & T \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Using the truth table, we can see that the given statement is equivalent to $$(\sim p) \vee(\sim q)$$.

Alternate Method :

The given statement $$(p \wedge(\sim q)) \vee((\sim p) \wedge q) \vee((\sim p) \wedge(\sim q))$$ can be simplified using the following steps:

$$ \begin{aligned} &(p \wedge(\sim q)) \vee((\sim p) \wedge q) \vee((\sim p) \wedge(\sim q)) \\\\ &= \left(p \wedge(\sim q)\right) \vee \left((\sim p) \wedge (q \vee (\sim q))\right) \\\\ &= \left(p \wedge(\sim q)\right) \vee \left((\sim p) \wedge t\right) \\\\ &= \left(p \wedge(\sim q)\right) \vee (\sim p) \\\\ &= (\sim p) \vee \left(p \wedge (\sim q)\right) \\\\ &= (\sim p \vee p) \wedge (\sim p \vee \sim q) \\\\ &= t \wedge (\sim p \vee \sim q) \\\\ &= \sim p \vee \sim q \end{aligned} $$

Therefore, the given statement is equivalent to $$(\sim p) \vee (\sim q)$$.

questionText: Negation of $p \wedge(q \wedge \sim(p \wedge q))$ is :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given statement is:

$p \wedge (q \wedge \sim(p \wedge q))$

We want to find the negation of this statement. To do that, we negate the whole expression:

$\sim (p \wedge (q \wedge \sim(p \wedge q)))$

Now, we will use De Morgan's laws to simplify the expression. De Morgan's laws state:

  1. $\sim (A \wedge B) \equiv \sim A \vee \sim B$
  2. $\sim (A \vee B) \equiv \sim A \wedge \sim B$

Applying the first De Morgan's law to the outer conjunction of the negated expression:

$\sim p \vee \sim (q \wedge \sim (p \wedge q))$

Now we need to address the inner conjunction $(q \wedge \sim (p \wedge q))$. To simplify this part of the expression, we again apply the first De Morgan's law:

$\sim p \vee (\sim q \vee (p \wedge q))$

Distribute the disjunction over the inner conjunction:

$$ \sim \mathrm{p} \vee((\sim \mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{p}) \wedge(\sim \mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{q})) $$

Apply the law of excluded middle (A ∨ ¬A = T) on the last term:

$$ \sim \mathrm{p} \vee(\sim \mathrm{q} \vee \mathrm{p}) $$

Apply De Morgan's law on the inner disjunction:

$$ \sim(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q}) \vee \mathrm{p} $$

Now, comparing this with the provided options, it matches with Option B :

$(\sim(p \wedge q)) \vee p$

So, the negation of the given statement is :

$(\sim(p \wedge q)) \vee p$

questionText:
The number of choices for $$\Delta \in \{ \wedge , \vee , \Rightarrow , \Leftrightarrow \} $$, such that

$$(p\Delta q) \Rightarrow ((p\Delta \sim q) \vee (( \sim p)\Delta q))$$ is a tautology, is :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $x:(p \Delta q) \Rightarrow(p \Delta \sim q) \vee(\sim p \Delta q)$

Case-I

When $\Delta$ is same as $v$

Then $(p \Delta \sim q) \vee(\sim p \Delta q)$ becomes

$(p \vee \sim q) \vee(\sim p \vee q)$ which is always true, so $x$ becomes a tautology.

Case-II

When $\Delta$ is same as $\wedge$

Then $(p \wedge q) \Rightarrow(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$

If $p \wedge q$ is $T$, then $(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$ is $F$

so $x$ cannot be a tautology.

Case-III

When $\Delta$ is same as $\Rightarrow$

Then $(p \Rightarrow \sim q) \vee(\sim p \Rightarrow q)$ is same at $(\sim p \vee \sim q) \vee$ $(p \vee q)$, which is always true, so $x$ becomes a tautology.

Case-IV

When $\Delta$ is same as $\Leftrightarrow$

Then $(p \Leftrightarrow q) \Rightarrow(p \Leftrightarrow \sim q) \vee(\sim p \Leftrightarrow q)$

$p \Leftrightarrow q$ is true when $p$ and $q$ have same truth values, then $p \Leftrightarrow \sim q$ and $\sim p \Leftrightarrow q$ both are false. Hence $x$ cannot be a tautology.

So finally $x$ can be $\vee$ or $\Rightarrow$.

solution:
There are total 9 compound propositions, out of which 6 contain $\sim s$. So if we assign $s$ as false, these 6 propositions will be true.

questionText: If q is false and p $$ \wedge $$ q $$ \leftrightarrow $$ r  is true, then which one of the following statements is a tautology  ?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given q is F and (p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \leftrightarrow $$ r is T

$$ \Rightarrow $$  p $$ \wedge $$ q is F which implies that r is F

$$ \Rightarrow $$  q is F and r is F

$$ \Rightarrow $$  (p $$ \wedge $$ r) is always F

$$ \Rightarrow $$  (p $$ \wedge $$ r) $$ \to $$ (p $$ \vee $$ r) is tautology.

questionText: The Boolean expression ((p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \vee $$ (p $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q)) $$ \wedge $$ ($$ \sim $$ p $$ \wedge $$ $$ \sim $$ q) is equivalent to :
subject: Mathematics
solution:
((p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \vee $$ (p $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q)) $$ \wedge $$ ($$ \sim $$ p $$ \wedge $$ $$ \sim $$ q)

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {\left( {\left( {p \vee \left( {p \vee \sim q} \right)} \right)} \right) \wedge \left( {q \vee \left( {p \vee \sim q} \right)} \right)} \right) \wedge \left( { \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {\left( {p \vee \sim q} \right) \wedge \left( {q \vee \sim q \vee p} \right)} \right) \wedge \left( { \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {\left( {p \vee \sim q} \right) \wedge \left( {t \vee p} \right)} \right) \wedge \left( { \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {\left( {p \vee \sim q} \right) \wedge t} \right) \wedge \left( { \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {p \vee \sim q} \right) \wedge \left( { \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {p \wedge \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right) \vee \left( { \sim q \wedge \sim p \wedge \sim q} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( {f \wedge \sim q} \right) \vee \left( { \sim q \wedge \sim p} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$f' \vee \left( { \sim q \wedge \sim p} \right)$$

$$ \equiv $$ $$\left( { \sim q \wedge \sim p} \right)$$

questionText:
Which one of the following statements is not a
tautology?
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Here you have to check all the options.

We know, p $$ \to $$ q = $$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ q

(A) (p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \to $$ (~ p) $$ \vee $$ q

= $$ \sim $$ (p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \vee $$ ((~ p) $$ \vee $$ q)

= ($$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q) $$ \vee $$ ((~ p) $$ \vee $$ q)

= $$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ (q $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q)

= $$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ t

[(q $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q) = t (tautology)]

= t

(B) (p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \to $$ p

= $$ \sim $$ (p $$ \wedge $$ q) $$ \vee $$ p

= $$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q $$ \vee $$ p

= t $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$ q

= t

(C) ( p $$ \vee $$ q) $$ \to $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ (~q))

= $$ \sim $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ q) $$ \vee $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ (~q))

= ($$ \sim $$ p $$ \wedge $$ $$ \sim $$ q) $$ \vee $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ (~q))

= $$ \sim $$ q $$ \wedge $$ (p $$ \vee $$ $$ \sim $$p)

= $$ \sim $$ q $$ \wedge $$ t

= $$ \sim $$ q (not tautology)

(D) p $$ \to $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ q)

= $$ \sim $$ p $$ \vee $$ ( p $$ \vee $$ q)

= t $$ \vee $$ q

= t

questionText:

Statement -I is False; Statement -II is True
Statement -I is True; Statement -II is True; Statement-II is a correct explanation for Statement-I
subject: Mathematics
solution:
Statement - I : $$(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge ( \sim p \wedge q)$$

p q $$ \sim p$$ $$ \sim q$$ $$p \wedge \sim q$$
 $$ \sim p \wedge q$$
 $$(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge ( \sim p \wedge q)$$
T T F F F F F
T F F T T F F
F T T F F T F
F F T T F F F

Hence, statement-I is a fallacy.

Statement - II : $$(p \to q) \leftrightarrow ( \sim q \to \sim p)$$

p q $$ \sim p$$ $$ \sim q$$ $$p \to q$$
 $$ \sim q \to \sim p$$
 $$(p \to q) \leftrightarrow ( \sim q \to \sim p)$$
T T F F T T T

Hence, statement - II is a tautology.

Now, statement-II does not depend on statement-I.
